edge: Video restoration aims to restore clear frames from degraded videos.
Key to video restoration depends on utilizing inter-frame information.
Existing deep learning methods often rely on complicated network architectures like optical flow estimation, deformable convolution, and cross-frame self-attention layers.
Proposed framework for video restoration is based on grouped spatial-temporal shift.
Grouped spatial shift helps attain expansive effective receptive fields.
Simple framework can effectively aggregate inter-frame information by introducing grouped spatial shift and basic 2D convolution.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms the previous state-of-the-art method on video deblurring and video denoising tasks.
Results indicate the potential to significantly reduce computational cost while maintaining high-quality results with the proposed approach.

We replace several 2D convolution blocks by stacking multiple grouped spatial-temporal shift (GSTS) blocks in the U-Net to effectively establish temporal correspondences and conduct multi-frame fusion.
The GSTS blocks are not applied at the finest scale to save computational cost. A GSTS block consists of three components: 1) a temporal shift, 2) a spatial shift, and 3) a lightweight fusion layer.

The spatial shift operation involves shifting the feature slice by a certain number of pixels in the x and y directions to obtain a shifted feature slice.
When the spatial shift causes void pixels in the border, they are set to zero to maintain the integrity of the shifted feature slice.
After shifting the feature group spatially, a depth-wise 3x3 convolution is applied to handle objects across the shifts and achieve smooth translation between adjacent shifted feature slices.
All the feature groups shifted spatially are concatenated along the channel dimension to obtain the spatially shifted feature for further processing and analysis.
